summary: Cathy O'Neil discusses Occupy the SEC's lawsuit against federal regulators for failing to implement the Volcker Rule on time, as required by Dodd-Frank. The court dismissed the case, ruling that citizens don't have legal standing to sue over regulatory delays—only the banks being regulated do. O'Neil argues this reveals a fundamental asymmetry in financial regulation: the people most affected by systemic risk (taxpayers who bail out banks) have no legal recourse to demand accountability, while the regulated entities control the conversation. She calls for better legal representation for average citizens in regulatory matters.
key_points: Occupy the SEC sued federal regulators for missing the deadline to implement the Volcker Rule, a key Dodd-Frank provision meant to function like a modern Glass-Steagall
The court ruled citizens don't have 'standing' to sue—only regulated banks can legally complain about regulations
This creates a structural problem: negative effects of poor regulation are so dispersed that individuals can't demonstrate sufficient harm to have legal recourse
Taxpayers who bail out banks paradoxically have no legal voice in how those banks are regulated
Class-action lawsuits are insufficient; there needs to be formal legal representation for taxpayers and systemic risk monitoring
money_quotes: They didn't have enough skin in the game, in other words. Because they're just, you know, citizens who care about having a functional regulatory environment.
That begs the question, who has skin in the regulation game? Answer: banks being regulated. So only those guys can complain to the courts about the regulation.

It seems like whenever I look around I see examples like this, where there are people getting away with crappy policies or even crappier deeds because it has a negative effect, but that negative effect is so dispersed that most people don't have enough 'standing' to sue.
Not to mention taxpayers who have bailed out the banks and want to avoid continuing doing that.
Who is looking out for the average non-insider? Who is keeping tabs on overall systemic risk?
In an ideal world that would exist inside the regulators themselves, but we all know it's not that ideal.
key_insight: The legal doctrine of 'standing' creates a perverse asymmetry where only banks can challenge financial regulations, while the dispersed taxpayers who bear systemic risk have no voice in the regulatory process.
